title: Bibliography
text:
Baskin, Carol C, and Jerry M Baskin. 2004. “Seeds: Ecology, Biogeography, and, Evolution of Dormancy and Germination.” Academic Press.


Garden, UBC Botanical. 2025. UBC Botanical Garden Climate Adaptation Plan.

title: Glossary of Terms
text:
Physical Dormancy

A type of dormancy where the seed coat is impermeable to water.

Stratification

The process of pretreating seeds to simulate natural winter conditions (moist-chilling).

Scarification




Weakening, opening, or otherwise altering the coat of a seed to encourage germination.

title: Castilleja victoriae
text: Family: Orobanchaceae Conservation Status: G1, Red Listed, Endangered

title: Castilleja victoriae
text:
Biological Context

Type: Hemiparasite (Facultative parasite).
Growth: Can survive independently but exhibits enhanced growth and reproduction when forming haustorial connections with a host plant.

title: Castilleja victoriae
text:
Propagation Challenges

Host Identity: The exact native host is explicitly unknown.
Hydrology: The host must tolerate the extreme seasonal hydrological cycle of vernal pools (winter saturation, summer drought).

title: Castilleja victoriae
text:
Propagation Protocol

Nursery Code: PD
Strategy: Polyculture approach recommended rather than reliance on a single host.
Co-Cultivation: Success requires simultaneous germination and co-development of the parasite and host.
Transplanting: Prick-out with the host.


Research Venue: Immediate initiation of Host Identification Trials. Focus on native GOE species found in vernal seeps, such as Festuca rubra (Red Fescue), native Trifolium species, and non-competitive native annuals.

title: Arctostaphylos uva-ursi
text: [cite_start]Family: Ericaceae [cite: 21] [cite_start]Conservation Status: G5 (Secure) [cite: 27]

title: Arctostaphylos uva-ursi
text:
Seed Morphology

[cite_start]Fruit Type: Drupe containing multiple nutlets[cite: 23, 25].
[cite_start]Dormancy: Physical (impermeable nutlet) + Physiological (embryo dormancy)[cite: 28, 32].
[cite_start]Inhibitors: The fruit pulp contains germination inhibitors; thorough cleaning is essential[cite: 28, 35].

title: Arctostaphylos uva-ursi
text:
Propagation Protocol

1. Fruit Processing

[cite_start]Cleaning: Fruit cleaning is essential to remove inhibitors[cite: 35].
[cite_start]Fermentation: Recent work suggests fermenting the fruit helps completely denature germination inhibitors, superior to straight mechanical cleaning[cite: 36].



2. Scarification

Method: Chemical scarification on the mixture of single-seed and coalesced nutlets.
Reagent: Concentrated Sulphuric Acid (\(H_2SO_4\)).
[cite_start]Duration: 30 minutes to 2 hours[cite: 37].
[cite_start]Post-Treatment: Rapid termination and neutralizing using a base (potassium bicarbonate), followed by a 24-hour water soak[cite: 38].



3. Stratification
[cite_start]Embryos require a “warm-moist” period followed by a “cold-moist” period[cite: 32]. 1. [cite_start]Warm Stratification: 60 days[cite: 39]. 2. [cite_start]Cold Stratification: 90 days[cite: 39].

title: Arctostaphylos uva-ursi
text:
Production Notes

[cite_start]Nursery Code: PY, PD[cite: 33].
[cite_start]Container: Use tall cells (conetainers) to avoid transplanting small plants[cite: 42].
[cite_start]Timeline: ~1.5 years from germination to production size[cite: 42].


[cite_start]Research Venue: Investigate sulphuric acid alternatives (enzymatic, biological) and the use of activated charcoal in media or single-cell sowing to test for allelopathy[cite: 43].

title: Meconella oregana
text: Family: Papaveraceae Conservation Status: G2, Red Listed, Endangered

title: Meconella oregana
text:
Seed Morphology

Fruit Type: Capsule
Seed Type: Tiny, smooth, shiny, and ovate to reniform (kidney-shaped).
Harvest Period: Late April

title: Meconella oregana
text:
Propagation Protocol

Nursery Code: PD, cool germinator.
Timing: Winter annual; requires cool germination.

title: Meconella oregana
text:
Conservation Strategy

Population Dynamics: Species exhibits extreme fluctuation (e.g., 60 to 1,209 individuals at a single site over consecutive years).
Ex Situ Management: The nursery must maintain a genetically diverse buffer stock, independent of unpredictable annual field census numbers.
Maternal Lines: Maternal lines might require constant maintenance due to low seed viability.

title: Meconella oregana
text:
Production Notes

Product Form: Plugs to be patched into appropriate microsites.
Habitat: Thin soils on rock outcrops, meadows, and bluffs.

title: Symphoricarpos albus
text: [cite_start]Family: Caprifoliaceae [cite: 63] [cite_start]Conservation Status: G5 (Secure) [cite: 69]

title: Symphoricarpos albus
text:
Seed Morphology

[cite_start]Fruit Type: Drupe [cite: 65]
[cite_start]Seed Type: Small, egg-shaped, somewhat flattened stone seeds [cite: 66]
[cite_start]Harvest Period: Fall (persistent fruit) [cite: 68]

title: Symphoricarpos albus
text:
Propagation Protocol

[cite_start]Nursery Code: PY, MD/PD [cite: 71]
Dormancy Type: Morphological Dormancy (MD). [cite_start]The main challenge is that the embryo is underdeveloped when the fruit is shed; only time can coax it to germinate[cite: 73].
[cite_start]Treatment: Despite being a taxon with a very hard seed coat, acid treatment may not be necessary—just time[cite: 74].
[cite_start]Key Consideration: Do not rush development; keep moist during treatment and use alternate temperature regimes[cite: 70].

title: Symphoricarpos albus
text:
Production Notes

[cite_start]Timeline: ~3 years to liner size[cite: 75].


[cite_start]Research Venue: Compare chemical stratification vs. none; investigate temperature regimes[cite: 76].

title: Species Database
Student Research Portal
text: Welcome HTP and Hort students! This project relies on your research to document the propagation protocols for British Columbia’s diverse flora.

title: Student Research Portal
text:
Instructions

Check the Board: Look at the “Live Claim Board” below. Find a species that is listed as “available” (Green).
Claim It: Type your name in the student-name column and change the status to “claimed” (Red).
Research: Download the template and complete the protocol using library resources and nursery records.
Submit: Upload your completed Word document via the submission link.

title: Student Research Portal
text:
1. Live Claim Board
Type directly in the sheet below to claim a species.

title: Silene spaldingii
text: [cite_start]Family: Caryophyllacea [cite: 97] [cite_start]Conservation Status: G2G3, Red Listed, Endangered [cite: 103]

title: Silene spaldingii
text:
Biological Challenges

[cite_start]Low Viability: Most work points towards obligate outcrossing as the source of poor seed set[cite: 107].
[cite_start]Deep Dormancy: Natural dormancy recorded to last up to 6 years (commonly 1–2 years)[cite: 110]. [cite_start]This is likely an evolutionary adaptation to stochastic environments[cite: 111].
[cite_start]Propagation Barrier: This multi-year dormancy limits the annual quantity of plants available for restoration and slows the turnover required to monitor ex situ genetic fidelity[cite: 112].

title: Silene spaldingii
text:
Propagation Protocol

[cite_start]Nursery Code: PD, 120d cs [cite: 105]
[cite_start]Dormancy Type: Deep physiological dormancy[cite: 111].
[cite_start]Chemical Treatment: Non-deep physiological dormancy is a good candidate for \(GA_3\) (Gibberellic acid) treatment to increase germination[cite: 108].
[cite_start]Cycling: Rigorous, standardized cycling is necessary to establish continuous stock production[cite: 113].

title: Silene spaldingii
text:
Genetic Management

[cite_start]Bagging: Directed self-crossing to try and increase seed set[cite: 109].
[cite_start]Manual Pollination: Controlled outcrossing via direct manual pollinating to rescue inbred populations[cite: 109].


[cite_start]Research Venue: \(GA_3\) applications; vegetative cloning for in-breeding recovery transplants[cite: 114].

title: Limnanthes macounii
text: [cite_start]Family: Limnanthaceae [cite: 118] [cite_start]Conservation Status: G2, Blue Listed, Special Concern [cite: 124]

title: Limnanthes macounii
text:
Seed Morphology

[cite_start]Fruit Type: Schizocarp [cite: 120]
[cite_start]Seed Type: Angular nutlet with a tuberculate (warty) tip[cite: 121, 125].
[cite_start]Dormancy: Exhibits strong dormancy[cite: 125].







The Hydrological Imperative



[cite_start]This species is an obligate winter annual adapted to vernal pools. Success depends on simulating this environment: * [cite_start]Cool Germination: Requires temperatures <10°C. * [cite_start]Winter Saturation: Soil must be saturated from October to March.

title: Limnanthes macounii
text:
Production Timeline
This species requires strict adherence to the seasonal cycle.
%%| fig-cap: “Annual Production Cycle for Limnanthes macounii” gantt title Vernal Pool Simulation Cycle dateFormat YYYY-MM-DD axisFormat %b
section Hydrology
Dry/Dormant       :done,    des-1, 2025-06-01, 2025-09-30
Saturation Period :active,  wet-1, 2025-10-01, 2026-03-31

section Nursery Work
Sow (Natural Cooling) :crit,    sow, 2025-09-01, 30d
Growth                :         grow, 2025-10-01, 2026-05-30
Senescence & Harvest  :         die, 2026-06-01, 30d

title: Abronia umbellata
text: [cite_start]Conservation Status: G4G5T2, Red Listed, Endangered [cite: 82] Ecological Notes: Coastal dunes, short-lived annual. [cite_start]Susceptible to disturbance[cite: 90].

title: Abronia umbellata
text:
Seed Morphology

[cite_start]Fruit Type: Anthocarp with achene [cite: 78]
[cite_start]Challenge: The seed (achene) is enclosed within a woody structure derived from the parent plant (the anthocarp)[cite: 86].
[cite_start]Risks: Strong mechanical forces used to break the anthocarp can destroy the embryo[cite: 88].

title: Abronia umbellata
text:
Propagation Protocol
[cite_start]Nursery Code: PY, PD [cite: 84]

Method A: Mechanical Scarification (Standardized)

[cite_start]Equipment: Industrial rock/sand tumbler[cite: 92].
[cite_start]Media: Mix achenes with coarse beach sand and fine grit[cite: 93].
[cite_start]Duration: Optimize through small-scale trials; initial trial duration set at 15 minutes[cite: 93].



Method B: Chemical Alternative

[cite_start]If mechanical abrasion proves inconsistent, trials using diluted sulfuric acid treatments (e.g., 10–20 minutes) should be conducted as a rapid, high-yield alternative[cite: 94].

title: Abronia umbellata
text:
Production Notes

[cite_start]Media: Deep-plugs, sandy, low-nutrient media[cite: 89].

title: Juniperus scopulorum
text: [cite_start]Family: Cupressaceae [cite: 45] [cite_start]Conservation Status: G5 (Secure) [cite: 51]

title: Juniperus scopulorum
text:
Seed Morphology

[cite_start]Fruit Type: Fleshy cone [cite: 47]
[cite_start]Seed Type: Hard-shelled, unwinged [cite: 48]
[cite_start]Seeds per Fruit: 2 [cite: 49]
[cite_start]Harvest Period: September - October [cite: 50]

title: Juniperus scopulorum
text:
Propagation Challenges
[cite_start]Propagating from seed is a difficult and lengthy process, primarily due to a complex “double dormancy”[cite: 55]. 1. [cite_start]Physical Dormancy: The seed possesses a hard, thick seed coat[cite: 56]. 2. [cite_start]Physiological Dormancy: The embryo contains “chemical factors” that require a long after-ripening period[cite: 56].

title: Juniperus scopulorum
text:
Protocol

[cite_start]Nursery Code: PY, PD, 120d ws, 150d cs [cite: 53]
[cite_start]Scarification Warning: Strong chemical scarification with strong acids like \(H_2SO_4\) is not recommended as it can carbonize and kill the seed[cite: 57].
[cite_start]Recommended Treatment: Some work suggests a Citric Acid soak (a light acid) can increase germination by removing inhibitors and softening the seed coat[cite: 58].
[cite_start]Timeline: ~2 years from germination[cite: 60].

title: Juniperus scopulorum
text:
Production Notes

[cite_start]Transplants fine and can be sown together in a seed pot (tray) and transferred to production pots post-germination[cite: 59].


[cite_start]Research Venue: Citric acid soak for seed scarification/inhibitor removal, and exposing the seed to plasma[cite: 61].

title: Project Overview
text:
This project serves as a living repository for germination protocols focused on British Columbia’s native seed plants. The primary objective is to move beyond simple viability testing and towards yield optimization—developing reliable, scalable methods for conservation and restoration.
Data collected here is intended to support the Provincial Seed Bank by documenting: 1. Germination Yield: Maximizing the conversion of stored seed to viable liner plants. 2. Protocol Reliability: Establishing standardized methods for diverse taxa. 3. Resource Management: Reducing the waste of valuable wild-collected genetic material.

title: Project Overview
text:
This project serves as a living repository for germination protocols focused on British Columbia’s native seed plants. The primary objective is to move beyond simple viability testing and towards yield optimization—developing reliable, scalable methods for conservation and restoration.
Data collected here is intended to support the Provincial Seed Bank by documenting: 1. Germination Yield: Maximizing the conversion of stored seed to viable liner plants. 2. Protocol Reliability: Establishing standardized methods for diverse taxa. 3. Resource Management: Reducing the waste of valuable wild-collected genetic material.

title: Project Overview
text:
The Challenge of Diversity
Native propagation is not a monolith. Each species presented in this database has been selected because it demonstrates a specific biological barrier or propagation challenge. By solving these specific “puzzle pieces,” we build a broader toolkit for the province’s flora.

Key Biological Barriers



🔒 Physical Dormancy
Challenge: Impermeable seed coats that prevent water uptake. Demonstrator Species: Arctostaphylos uva-ursi Focus: Acid scarification limits and mechanical alternatives.


🧩 Complex Dormancy
Challenge: Seeds requiring multiple environmental cues (e.g., warm then cold periods) or chemical triggers. Demonstrator Species: Juniperus scopulorum Focus: Mitigating “double dormancy” without damaging embryos.


🤝 Ecological Dependence
Challenge: Species that require biological partners to thrive. Demonstrator Species: Castilleja victoriae Focus: Host-parasite co-cultivation and hydrological simulation.


📉 Low Viability & Inbreeding
Challenge: Rare taxa with compromised genetic health or “grouchy” seed sets. Demonstrator Species: Silene spaldingii Focus: Maximizing yield from limited, high-value inputs.

title: Project Overview
text:
Using This Guide
This documentation is structured to provide both theoretical background and actionable protocols.

Methodologies: General techniques applicable across genera (e.g., plasma treatment, stratification).
Species Profiles: Detailed, taxon-specific recipes including phenology, harvest timing, and nursery codes.

title: Project Overview
text:
Get Involved
Are you a student or researcher interested in contributing? We are actively seeking collaborators to help document the 935+ taxa in our collection.






Student Researchers



Visit the Student Portal to claim a species and access the research templates.

title: Methodologies
text:
To standardize treatment protocols, the Nursery utilizes the Nikolaeva-Baskin Classification System. Unlike descriptive systems, this approach targets the specific mechanism preventing germination.








Code
Type
Mechanism




ND
No Dormancy
Embryo is mature and ready to germinate.


PY
Physical
Water-impermeable coat prevents uptake.


PD
Physiological
Internal chemical factors inhibit germination.


MD
Morphological
Embryo is underdeveloped at dispersal.


MPD
Morphophysiological
Combination of underdeveloped embryo and physiological inhibition.

title: Methodologies
text:
To standardize treatment protocols, the Nursery utilizes the Nikolaeva-Baskin Classification System. Unlike descriptive systems, this approach targets the specific mechanism preventing germination.








Code
Type
Mechanism




ND
No Dormancy
Embryo is mature and ready to germinate.


PY
Physical
Water-impermeable coat prevents uptake.


PD
Physiological
Internal chemical factors inhibit germination.


MD
Morphological
Embryo is underdeveloped at dispersal.


MPD
Morphophysiological
Combination of underdeveloped embryo and physiological inhibition.

title: Methodologies
text:
Seed Coat Treatments (PY)
Treatments targeting Physical Dormancy (PY) involve modifying the seed coat to allow water and oxygen penetration.

Mechanical Scarification
Physically damaging the seed coat.

Sandpaper Abrasion: Effective for seeds large enough to hold. Note: Change sandpaper regularly as debris accumulates.
Nicking: Using a scalpel to breach the coat. Target: Water gaps (natural weak points). Common Usage: Large legumes like Lupinus.
Tumblers: For large batches, industrial rock tumblers with coarse grit are used (See species profiles for duration).



Chemical Scarification
Using corrosive agents to etch the coat.

title: Methodologies
text:
Safety First
Sulfuric Acid (\(H_2SO_4\)): The primary chemical scarification agent at the Nursery due to efficiency. Used for Arctostaphylos. Requires strict safety protocols.

title: Methodologies
text:
Physiological Treatments (PD/MPD)
Treatments addressing internal chemical dormancy or embryo development.

Hydropriming
Controlled hydration to activate metabolic processes without full germination.

Cold Water Soak: 10-15°C for >12 hours. Softens coats and hydrates.
Warm Water Soak: 25-40°C. Promotes absorption in species requiring moderate heat.
Constant Water Drip: Continuous supply to wash away surface inhibitors.



Growth Regulators
Hormonal applications to stimulate embryo growth.

Smoke Primer: Infused water containing karrikins. Target: Fire-adapted species (California chaparral, Mediterranean).
Gibberellic Acid (\(GA_3\)): Stimulates embryo growth for deep dormancy. Warning: Monitor concentration to avoid adverse seedling effects.
Potassium Nitrate (\(KNO_3\)): 0.2-0.3% soak. Stimulates metabolic activity.



Osmotic Priming
Research Application: Soaking in low water potential solutions (PEG or Salts) to simulate drought/stress. * Note: Rarely used at the Nursery due to limited need for PEG6000 applications.

title: Methodologies
text:
Environmental Stratification
Simulating seasonal cycles to break complex dormancy.






Nursery Locations




Cold Stratification (1-5°C): Production House (winter) or Header House Refrigerator.
Warm Stratification (15-25°C): Heat bench in 7A (Feb–June) or Production House Seed Table (June–Oct).
Alternating Temps: Production House North Cold Frames (bottom heat cycled on/off).

title: Methodologies
text:
Seedling Support & Ecology
treatments applied at sowing or post-germination to ensure establishment.

Inoculants

Mycorrhizal: Fungi introduced to enhance nutrient uptake (Phosphorus). Crucial for Ericaceae.
Bacterial: Rhizobium introduction for nitrogen-fixing legumes.



Ecological Interactions

Hemiparasite Co-cultivation: Simultaneous germination of Castilleja spp. with native hosts to form haustorial connections.
Allelopathy Mitigation: Using activated charcoal or single-cell sowing to prevent infraspecific chemical warfare (e.g., Arctostaphylos).
